title: Prompting For Useful Work
summary: Turn vague requests into clear, repeatable prompt patterns that produce work-ready drafts instead of generic filler.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: The Four Inputs Of A Strong Prompt
lessons.subtitle: Task, audience, source material, and output shape.
lessons.objective: Use a simple structure for prompts that improves quality without overcomplicating the request.
lessons.useWhen: Task, audience, source material, and output shape.
lessons.whyItMatters: Most weak prompts fail in predictable ways: unclear job, missing context, no audience, or no output shape.

coreConcepts.title: Task
coreConcepts.text: Say exactly what needs to be produced and what decision or action it should support.

coreConcepts.title: Source Material
coreConcepts.text: Provide the raw material, approved facts, or examples the model should rely on.

coreConcepts.title: Output Shape
coreConcepts.text: State the format you want so the draft arrives closer to how you need to use it.

workedExample.scenario: A user asks for "a better summary" and gets a vague paragraph.
workedExample.approach: They specify audience, source notes, a three-bullet structure, and the decision the summary should support.
workedExample.result: The output becomes clear and directly usable.
workedExample.verification: They check whether every bullet is supported by the provided notes.

doThisNow.task: Rewrite one of your existing prompts using the prompt skeleton.
steps: Paste only the relevant source material.
Add a format and one review rule.

commonMistakes: Trying to sound smart instead of specific.
Leaving the model to guess the audience.
Not asking for an explicit structure.

roleVariants.manager: Use AI to shorten prep, synthesis, and communication loops without removing judgment.
roleVariants.writer: Push for clearer source material, tighter briefs, and reusable language patterns.
roleVariants.analyst: Separate analysis from storytelling so you can verify numbers before sharing narratives.
roleVariants.operator: Build repeatable handoff-ready outputs instead of one-off conversations.

sourceNotes: Reviewed for business IC relevance and durable workflow guidance.
Keep verification and organizational policy checks explicit in future revisions.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Roles And Perspective
lessons.subtitle: A role is useful when it sharpens judgment, not when it adds theater.
lessons.objective: Use role instructions to improve perspective and quality rather than to create empty prompt ritual.
lessons.useWhen: A role is useful when it sharpens judgment, not when it adds theater.
lessons.whyItMatters: A good role narrows the lens of the response. A bad role just decorates the prompt without changing the work.

coreConcepts.title: Use Functional Roles
coreConcepts.text: Ask for the perspective that matters to the task: reviewer, planner, editor, or analyst.

coreConcepts.title: Tie The Role To Criteria
coreConcepts.text: Define what the role should optimize for, such as clarity, risk awareness, or audience fit.

coreConcepts.title: Keep The Role Lightweight
coreConcepts.text: One clear line about perspective is usually enough.

workedExample.scenario: A manager needs a difficult status update rewritten for senior leaders.
workedExample.approach: They ask the model to act as an executive editor focused on clarity, risk visibility, and brevity.
workedExample.result: The tone and structure improve because the role changes the evaluation standard.
workedExample.verification: The manager checks that the new draft still reflects the actual project status and does not soften key risks.

doThisNow.task: Add one functional role and one optimization rule to a prompt you already use.
steps: Define what that role should optimize for.

commonMistakes: Using a role with no evaluation criteria.
Adding dramatic persona language that does not affect the work.

roleVariants.manager: Use AI to shorten prep, synthesis, and communication loops without removing judgment.
roleVariants.writer: Push for clearer source material, tighter briefs, and reusable language patterns.
roleVariants.analyst: Separate analysis from storytelling so you can verify numbers before sharing narratives.
roleVariants.operator: Build repeatable handoff-ready outputs instead of one-off conversations.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Examples And Output Formats
lessons.subtitle: Examples are often the shortest path to better results.
lessons.objective: Use examples, structure, and formatting cues to make outputs more consistent and easier to review.
lessons.useWhen: Examples are often the shortest path to better results.
lessons.whyItMatters: When the model sees a good example or precise layout, it spends less effort guessing the form of the answer.

coreConcepts.title: Show, Do Not Only Tell
coreConcepts.text: A short example of the expected format usually improves output more than extra explanation.

coreConcepts.title: Separate Content From Shape
coreConcepts.text: Define the structure clearly so you can judge the substance independently.

coreConcepts.title: Consistency Helps Reuse
coreConcepts.text: Stable output formats are easier to turn into templates and team playbooks.

workedExample.scenario: An analyst needs weekly updates in the same format every time.
workedExample.approach: They provide last week’s approved format as the model example and ask the model to reuse the structure only.
workedExample.result: The update becomes consistent and easier to compare week to week.
workedExample.verification: The analyst confirms the new draft did not carry over stale facts from the example.

artifact.label: Example
artifact.title: Formatting moves
items: Say which parts are structure only.
List any fields that must be updated from new source material.

doThisNow.task: Find one approved past document and use it as a format example in your next prompt.
steps: Highlight the structure you want to copy.
Tell the model not to reuse old facts.
Compare the final output against the original format.

commonMistakes: Providing examples without saying what to copy.
Letting the model reuse stale content.

roleVariants.analyst: Separate analysis from storytelling so you can verify numbers before sharing narratives.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Fixing A Weak Output
lessons.subtitle: Do not start over every time; diagnose what is missing.
lessons.objective: Use targeted follow-ups to improve low-quality drafts instead of repeatedly asking for a rewrite.
lessons.useWhen: Do not start over every time; diagnose what is missing.
lessons.whyItMatters: Most bad outputs fail for a small number of reasons. Diagnosing the failure saves time and teaches you which context matters most.

coreConcepts.title: Name The Failure
coreConcepts.text: Say whether the issue is missing context, wrong tone, weak structure, unsupported claims, or poor prioritization.

coreConcepts.title: Add One Constraint At A Time
coreConcepts.text: Change a small number of variables so you learn what actually improved the result.

coreConcepts.title: Reset When Needed
coreConcepts.text: If the thread is noisy or confused, restate the task in a clean prompt instead of patching endlessly.

workedExample.scenario: A draft memo is too long and buries the decision.
workedExample.approach: The user asks for a rewrite that leads with the decision, limits the memo to five bullets, and marks any unsupported claims.
workedExample.result: The second draft is much easier to use because the failure mode was explicitly corrected.
workedExample.verification: The user checks that the shorter version still preserves the key facts from the source notes.

doThisNow.task: Take one weak AI output and improve it with a diagnostic follow-up instead of a generic rewrite request.
steps: Decide whether the thread should be reset.

commonMistakes: Saying "make it better" with no diagnosis.
Changing too many variables at once.
Continuing a broken thread too long.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Building A Reusable Prompt Library
lessons.subtitle: Save patterns, not just good prompts.
lessons.objective: Turn successful prompts into reusable assets that are easier to maintain and teach.
lessons.useWhen: Save patterns, not just good prompts.
lessons.whyItMatters: A prompt library compounds your learning. It reduces rework and gives teammates a starting point that already reflects your standards.

coreConcepts.title: Save The Context Pattern
coreConcepts.text: What matters most is often the structure of the request and review rules, not the exact wording.

coreConcepts.title: Store An Example Output
coreConcepts.text: A prompt paired with a good sample is easier to reuse than a prompt alone.

coreConcepts.title: Note The Review Standard
coreConcepts.text: Capture what still had to be checked or edited so others understand the limits.

workedExample.scenario: A coordinator keeps rebuilding the same meeting follow-up prompt from scratch.
workedExample.approach: They save the context block, the output format, and the review notes from a successful version.
workedExample.result: Future follow-ups become faster and more consistent.
workedExample.verification: They confirm the saved prompt works on a second meeting before calling it reusable.

doThisNow.task: Create one prompt library entry from a prompt that worked well this month.
steps: Note what still required human review.

commonMistakes: Assuming a prompt is reusable after one success.

outcomes: Write clearer prompts for real work.
Turn successful prompts into reusable patterns.
nextModuleBridge: Next, apply those prompting skills with stronger safety, privacy, and verification habits.
